intro_band.agent_remit_summary: OpenHands is an autonomous software-engineering agent that accepts natural-language tasks and completes them end-to-end by writing and running code inside a per-conversation sandboxed runtime. This remit covers the deployment's control-plane half — the FastAPI app server that authenticates users, provisions sandboxes, brokers secrets to the sandbox, stores conversation events, and mediates Git / issue-tracker integration calls — while the agentic core (the `CodeActAgent` and its tool runtime) is a separately-packaged SDK. The app server must keep each conversation's state, events and credentials isolated, deliver secrets only over a dual-authenticated SaaS-to-sandbox path, keep secret values out of logs and Git, and re-authenticate integration calls per request against the requesting user's scoped credentials.
intro_band.agent_structure_summary: Python FastAPI application under `openhands/app_server/` (the legacy `openhands/server/` modules are now thin re-exports of it). Routers mount under `/api/v1` via `v1_router`, guarded by a shared `get_dependencies()` that attaches a session-key check only when `SESSION_API_KEY` is set; the OSS `DefaultUserAuth` returns `user_id=None`, so the default single-user deployment runs the control plane with no authentication. Secrets and settings persist through file-backed stores (`file_secrets_store.py`, `file_settings_store.py`) that serialize with `expose_secrets=True`, while a `JwtService` provides JWE (algorithm-pinned) and per-sandbox `os.urandom(32)` session keys. Sandboxes are provisioned by `DockerSandboxService` (or a bare-subprocess `ProcessSandboxService`); `docker-compose.yml` binds port 3000 and mounts the host Docker socket. Log redaction (`SensitiveDataFilter`, `RedactURLParamsFilter`) and a durable per-conversation event store (`EventService`) are wired throughout.

behavior_summary:
The dominant pattern is a control plane with genuinely careful controls on its most sensitive path but an open front door by default. Raw secret delivery to the sandbox is dual-gated (per-sandbox session key plus a RUNNING-sandbox check in session_auth.py), integrations re-authenticate per request with user-scoped tokens, logs are redacted, and dependencies are exactly pinned — yet get_dependencies() attaches no authentication in the default OSS configuration and LocalhostCORSMiddleware reflects any origin with credentials when CORS origins are unconfigured.

Read the two front-door gaps together: an unauthenticated /api/v1 plane reachable cross-origin from any web page (or over the network under the recommended 0.0.0.0 bind) lets a caller start conversations that execute code in the sandbox and act with the user's stored Git tokens. Because the agentic core's isolation, tool-gating and injection handling live in the out-of-scope SDK, several of the strongest remit clauses are Enforcement-Not-Possible from this snapshot; the operative in-scope gaps are the default auth posture, plaintext secret storage at rest, and the unsandboxed process backend.

rules.rule_text: Execute agent-generated shell commands, Python / IPython, and other language runtimes inside the sandboxed runtime only, using the enabled agent tools (command, editor, Jupyter, browser, think, finish).
Read, write, create, edit, and delete files only within the per-conversation sandbox workspace.
Record every action — the tool invoked, its arguments, the outcome, and the timestamp — to a durable per-conversation event store.
Halt cleanly when a configured per-conversation cap (budget, iteration/step count, wall-clock, or max age) is reached.
Executing agent-generated code, or reading/writing files, on the host operating system outside the sandboxed runtime.
Sharing state, memory, conversation events, or credentials across users or across conversations/sessions.
Auto-upgrading its own code, the pinned SDK packages, or its dependencies at runtime.
Event webhooks / callbacks (app server → external subscriber) Yes No Operator-registered; deliveries must be authenticated.
User-configured secrets held in the secrets store (must be encrypted at rest and returned masked by default).
API keys, tokens, or secret values written into logs, error messages, trajectories, or the model's context window.
Raw secret values returned to, or routed through, the SDK client rather than delivered SaaS→sandbox under the required dual authentication (Bearer token and an active-sandbox session key).
Any conversation's state, memory, events, or credentials crossing into another conversation or another user.
Secrets, credentials, tokens, or `.env` file contents committed to any Git branch.

rules.section: Action Boundaries — Requires Human Approval Before Execution
Action Boundaries — Requires Human Approval Before Execution
rules.rule_text: Adding a new MCP tool server at runtime.
Any integration-admin capability: delete repository, remove webhook, transfer ownership, change billing, grant/revoke collaborator access.
Integration calls are re-authenticated per request against the requesting user's scoped credentials.
Each user is limited to a bounded number of concurrent conversations (default 3), each with its own isolated sandbox.

findings.summary: The V1 control-plane API applies no authentication in the default OSS configuration, leaving sandbox and conversation control open to any reachable caller.
findings.description: `get_dependencies()` attaches the `X-Session-API-Key` check only when the `SESSION_API_KEY` env var is set; with no key and non-SAAS mode (the OSS default) it returns an empty list, and `DefaultUserAuth.get_user_id()` always returns None. The documented `uvicorn --host 0.0.0.0` bind and the compose `3000:3000` mapping make the open plane network-reachable, so a caller can start conversations (which execute code in the sandbox) and act with stored Git tokens. Authentication is an opt-in extension point (`SESSION_API_KEY` or an enterprise `user_auth_class`), not a default.

evidence.snippet: get_dependencies() appends check_session_api_key only if _SESSION_API_KEY is set; else (OSS default, non-SAAS) returns [] — no auth dependency on /api/v1 routers
DefaultUserAuth.get_user_id() returns None always; single unauthenticated root user, amplified by server/__main__.py recommending --host 0.0.0.0 and docker-compose.yml binding 3000:3000

recommended_actions: Default the control plane to authenticated: require `SESSION_API_KEY` (or an equivalent auth layer) at startup and refuse to serve non-loopback binds without one, rather than silently returning an empty dependency list in `get_dependencies()`.
Bind to `127.0.0.1` by default in the shipped `docker-compose.yml` / launch docs and document the network-exposure risk of `0.0.0.0` explicitly.

findings.summary: When no CORS origins are configured (the default), the middleware reflects any request origin while allowing credentials, enabling cross-origin browser access to the API.
findings.description: `LocalhostCORSMiddleware.is_allowed_origin()` returns True for any origin whenever `permitted_cors_origins` is empty — the default from `get_default_permitted_cors_origins()` — and the middleware is constructed with `allow_credentials=True`. A malicious web page the user visits can therefore make credentialed cross-origin requests to a locally-bound instance; combined with the default no-auth plane (PRAX-2026-07-17-001) this yields a browser-driven path to conversation-start and in-sandbox code execution.

evidence.snippet: is_allowed_origin() returns True for any origin when allow_origins is empty ("Allow any origin when no specific origins are configured"), while __init__ sets allow_credentials=True
get_default_permitted_cors_origins() returns [] by default, so the reflect-any-origin branch is the shipped behavior unless OH_PERMITTED_CORS_ORIGINS is set

recommended_actions: Default to a deny-all (or localhost-exact) CORS policy: never combine origin reflection with `allow_credentials=True`, and require explicit `OH_PERMITTED_CORS_ORIGINS` for any non-localhost origin.
Emit a startup error (not just a per-request warning) when credentials are allowed with an empty origin allowlist.

findings.summary: The OSS file-backed secrets and settings stores persist provider tokens, custom secrets, and LLM keys to disk in plaintext.
findings.description: `FileSecretsStore.store()` writes `secrets.json` and `FileSettingsStore.store()` writes `settings.json` via `model_dump_json(context={'expose_secrets': True})`, and the master key file `.keys` is likewise persisted in cleartext. The remit requires user secrets be encrypted at rest; a `JwtService.encrypt_value()` JWE helper exists but the file stores do not use it. Values are correctly masked on read (SecretStr / listing without value), so this is an at-rest gap, not a disclosure-on-read gap.
policy_rule_text: User-configured secrets held in the secrets store (must be encrypted at rest and returned masked by default).

evidence.snippet: store() serializes Secrets with expose_secrets=True and writes plaintext to secrets.json; settings/file_settings_store.py:48-52 does the same for settings.json (LLM keys)
generated master key persisted to <persistence_dir>/.keys with context={'expose_secrets': True} — plaintext key at rest, while JwtService.encrypt_value() (JWE) is available but unused by these stores

recommended_actions: Encrypt secret and settings values at rest with the existing `JwtService.encrypt_value()` (JWE) before writing `secrets.json` / `settings.json`, decrypting on load.
Restrict permissions on the persistence directory (`.keys`, `secrets.json`, `settings.json`) to the server user and document the encryption-at-rest expectation for custom `FileStore` backends.

findings.summary: The process/local sandbox backend provisions the agent server as a bare host subprocess with no container isolation boundary.
findings.description: When `RUNTIME=local`/`process`, `ProcessSandboxService` spawns the agent server via `subprocess` in a working directory on the host rather than inside a container, so the "sandbox" the remit relies on as the execution and isolation boundary is only OS-process separation. Agent file I/O and command execution then reach the host filesystem, which the remit forbids outside the sandbox. The choice of backend is operator-selected and the execution enforcement itself lives in the out-of-scope agent-server, but the app server here is what provisions the no-isolation environment.
policy_rule_text: Executing agent-generated code, or reading/writing files, on the host operating system outside the sandboxed runtime.

evidence.snippet: ProcessSandboxService spawns each sandbox as a separate agent-server host process in a dedicated directory — no container/namespace isolation; selected via RUNTIME=local/process (config.py:339)

recommended_actions: Treat the process/local backend as development-only: refuse to start it unless an explicit opt-in flag is set, and warn loudly that it provides no host isolation.
Document that Docker/Remote/Kubernetes backends are the only ones satisfying the remit's host-isolation requirement, and default deployments to a containerized backend.

findings.summary: The agent-server sandbox image is pulled by a mutable tag rather than a pinned digest, and its version skews from the exactly-pinned SDK.
findings.description: `docker-compose.yml` defaults `AGENT_SERVER_IMAGE_TAG` to `1.30.0-python`, a mutable tag resolved at pull time with no digest pin, so the image that actually runs agent code can change under the same tag. It also skews from the Python SDK packages, which are exactly pinned to `==1.36.0` in `pyproject.toml`. The remit names supply-chain integrity of the pinned components as a risk sensitivity; a mutable image tag is the weakest link in an otherwise well-pinned stack.

evidence.snippet: AGENT_SERVER_IMAGE_TAG defaults to 1.30.0-python (mutable tag, no digest) — the image running agent code can change under the tag
openhands-sdk/agent-server/tools pinned ==1.36.0 while the compose agent-server image tag is 1.30.0-python — pin discipline present in Python deps but not the sandbox image

recommended_actions: Pin the agent-server image by digest (`ghcr.io/openhands/agent-server@sha256:...`) or at minimum align its tag with the SDK version and verify it at pull.
Add image provenance/signature verification (e.g. cosign) to the pull path for the sandbox image.

findings.summary: The HTTP rate limiter is in-memory, per-process, and keyed on the client host, so it is bypassable and does not hold across workers or behind a proxy.
findings.description: `RateLimitMiddleware` uses an `InMemoryRateLimiter` (10 req/s) keyed on `request.client.host`. The counter lives in process memory, so each uvicorn worker or replica enforces its own independent budget, and keying on the peer address means a reverse proxy collapses all clients to one bucket (over-throttling) or, if forwarded addresses are honored upstream, an attacker can rotate the key. It is a real control for a single-process localhost instance but does not provide a durable, deployment-wide limit.

evidence.snippet: InMemoryRateLimiter.__call__ keys history on request.client.host in a per-process defaultdict; RateLimitMiddleware wired in app.py:83 with requests=10/s — no shared/distributed store

recommended_actions: Back the rate limiter with a shared store (e.g. Redis, already used elsewhere in `utils/redis.py`) so limits hold across workers and replicas.
Derive the client key from a trusted forwarded-for chain (or authenticated identity) rather than the raw peer address when running behind a proxy.

positives.title: Dual-authenticated, running-sandbox-only secret delivery
positives.description: Raw secret values are returned to the sandbox only over an `X-Session-API-Key` that is validated against a RUNNING sandbox and its owner, so a leaked key cannot be replayed after the sandbox is paused, stopped, or deleted.

positives.title: Per-request re-authentication of integrations with user-scoped tokens
positives.description: Git/issue integration calls build a fresh `ProviderHandler` from the requesting user's scoped provider tokens on each request rather than reusing a shared client, matching the remit's per-request re-auth expectation.

positives.title: Secret redaction in logs
positives.description: `SensitiveDataFilter` scrubs env-derived and pattern-matched secrets (plus SDK literal/dict redaction) and `RedactURLParamsFilter` strips `session_api_key` from uvicorn access logs, keeping credentials out of the log surface.

positives.title: Durable structured per-conversation event store
positives.description: `EventService.save_event` persists every conversation event to a durable backend (filesystem/S3/GCS) as the webhook `/events` endpoint receives them, giving an action-level record sufficient to reconstruct a conversation.

positives.title: Exact dependency pinning with committed lockfiles
positives.description: The SDK trio (`==1.36.0`), `litellm==1.84.1` and `openai==2.33.0` are exactly pinned and both `poetry.lock` and `uv.lock` are committed, giving strong supply-chain reproducibility.

positives.title: JWE algorithm pinning and generated master key
positives.description: `JwtService` restricts JWE to `dir`+`A256GCM` via a fixed registry to block cryptographic-agility attacks, and the master key is auto-generated from `os.urandom(32)` rather than hardcoded when no `JWT_SECRET` or key file is present.

positives.title: Structured secret-name governance
positives.description: `constants.py` enforces blocked secret names/prefixes (protecting internal container env and `LLM_*` controls), an explicit overridable list, and count/length size limits before secrets are accepted.

rows.purpose: durable action-level record of every conversation event (tool calls, observations, state updates)
application/diagnostic log; daily rotation, 7 backups; written only when LOG_TO_FILE/DEBUG is enabled
LLM prompt/response capture for debugging; opt-in via LOG_TO_FILE (SensitiveDataFilter not attached to these loggers)

raise_posture.weighted_rationale: Partial overall. OpenHands' app server runs real, code-level Zero-Trust machinery — dual-authenticated secret delivery, running-sandbox key invalidation, per-request integration re-auth, JWE with algorithm pinning — alongside strong supply-chain hygiene (exact SDK/LiteLLM pins plus committed lockfiles) and a durable, redacted, structured event record. What holds the score down is a permissive default posture (no authentication on the control plane and wildcard-with-credentials CORS out of the box) and plaintext secret storage at rest, while the agent-core controls that would lift Zero Trust and Domain further are out of scope and score Enforcement-Not-Possible.

categories.name: Limit Your Domain
categories.rationale: The app server presents a bounded, well-typed REST surface with scoped per-provider MCP tools (`create_pr` opens drafts, no merge tool) and structured secret-name governance (blocked names/prefixes, overridable list) in `constants.py`; the agent's topical scope is enforced in the out-of-scope SDK.

categories.name: Balance Your Knowledge Base
categories.rationale: Secret brokering into the sandbox is careful (session-key gated, masked on read) but user secrets and settings are written to disk in plaintext by the file stores, and the untrusted-content handling the remit cares about (web/issue/microagent) is enforced in the out-of-scope agentic core.

categories.name: Implement Zero Trust
categories.rationale: Real interposition exists where it runs — dual-auth secret retrieval, RUNNING-only session keys, ownership checks, per-request token resolution — but `get_dependencies()` attaches no auth in the default OSS config and `LocalhostCORSMiddleware` reflects any origin with credentials when unconfigured, leaving the control plane open by default.

categories.name: Manage Your Supply Chain
categories.rationale: The SDK trio (`openhands-sdk`/`agent-server`/`tools` `==1.36.0`), `litellm==1.84.1` and `openai==2.33.0` are exactly pinned with committed `poetry.lock` and `uv.lock`; the notable gap is the mutable `AGENT_SERVER_IMAGE_TAG` (default `1.30.0-python`) pulled by tag rather than digest.

categories.name: Build an AI Red Team
categories.rationale: Defensive comments show threat-aware design (algorithm-agility prevention, leaked-key-after-pause rejection, URL-param redaction), but no adversarial-testing artifact is visible in scope (`tests/` is excluded), so there is no evidence of a red-team feedback loop driving the design.

categories.name: Monitor Continuously
categories.rationale: A durable per-conversation event store (`EventService.save_event`, persisted on every webhook `/events` delivery) provides a structured, action-level record, and `SensitiveDataFilter` + `RedactURLParamsFilter` keep secrets out of logs; it is not tamper-evident and default file logging is opt-in.
